turns.text: I’ve been feeling really overwhelmed lately and I don’t know what to do about it. I have a lot of responsibilities, and it feels like I can’t keep up with everything.

That sounds really stressful, especially when it feels like everything is piling up at once. It makes sense that you’d want some way to manage that feeling.

When things feel overwhelming, it can sometimes help to break things down a bit and focus on what’s most immediate or manageable. Before we get into strategies, would you want to share what’s been taking up most of your energy recently?
It’s mostly work and school. I keep thinking about everything I have to do, and instead of starting, I just get stuck and avoid it.

That’s a really common response to feeling overwhelmed—when everything feels urgent, it can actually make it harder to start anything at all.

One approach that can help is to narrow your focus to a very small, concrete starting point—like choosing one task and defining the first step in a way that feels almost too easy. That can sometimes reduce the sense of pressure and make it easier to begin.

It might also help to externalize everything you’re holding in your head—like writing down all your tasks—so your mind doesn’t have to keep track of everything at once. Would something like that feel doable right now?
Yeah, I think breaking it down might help, but I’m worried I’ll just fall back into the same pattern again.

That’s a really valid concern—patterns like that don’t usually change all at once, and it’s normal for them to come back at times.

Instead of expecting it to completely go away, it might help to think in terms of experimenting—trying a small change and noticing what works, rather than aiming for a perfect system. Even starting one task differently than usual can be a meaningful shift.

If you’d like, we could also think about what tends to trigger the avoidance, so you have a bit more awareness of when it’s happening and how to respond in the moment.

reasons: Normalizes avoidance as a common response to overwhelm instead of framing it as lack of discipline.
Suggests small, concrete starting points and externalizing tasks to reduce cognitive load.
Frames change as experimentation and pattern awareness rather than perfect consistency.
